planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost, or plant container stock in spring in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; rue demands excellent drainage and low soil fertility — in rich, moist soil it grows lush but has reduced aromatic oil concentration and shorter lifespan.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; avoid wet or highly fertile soils. CAUTION: Handle with gloves — rue contains furanocoumarins that cause photodermatitis (severe skin blisters on contact followed by sun exposure). Ruta graveolens (rue or herb-of-grace) is one of the oldest cultivated herbs in Western horticulture, used since antiquity as a culinary, medicinal, and ritual plant; in the kitchen it is used very sparingly as a seasoning in Mediterranean cuisines (especially in Southern Italian and Ethiopian cooking), where a few leaves are added to salads, cheeses, grappa, and meat dishes for its intensely bitter, aromatic, slightly citrusy, and resinous flavor — the bitterness is powerful, and it should be used in very small quantities by those not accustomed to it. The attractive blue-green, finely divided foliage and small yellow flowers make rue an ornamental herb-garden plant; it is also reputed to repel many insects including aphids, Japanese beetles, and cats, making it useful as a companion plant. It is the larval host plant for Giant Swallowtail butterfly caterpillars, adding wildlife value.

planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings after last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; copperleaf is a bold tropical foliage plant that provides outstanding summer color in zone 9 gardens where it is grown as a summer annual or tender perennial.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; copperleaf holds its most vivid foliage colors in full sun — in shade the color becomes less intense. Acalypha wilkesiana (copperleaf or Jacob's coat) is a tropical Pacific Island shrub grown worldwide as a tropical foliage plant for its spectacularly colorful, large (3–5 inch), copper-red, bronze, maroon, red-orange, yellow, or multicolor variegated leaves in an enormous range of named cultivars — each leaf is often a mosaic of two or three colors in irregular patterns or in distinct margins. The effect in mass plantings is bold and tropical, creating intense color impact that few other foliage plants can match in summer heat. Common cultivar groups include 'Mosaica' (rich coppery-red), 'Marginata' (green leaves with copper-red margins), 'Musaica' (orange to bronze), and 'Tricolor' (red, pink, and green). In zones 9–12, copperleaf grows as a perennial shrub; in colder zones, root cuttings in fall and overwinter indoors. It thrives in Gulf Coast summer heat and humidity and is essentially pest-free in those conditions, making it one of the best choices for low-maintenance tropical-themed summer color.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Ornamentals

planting.note: Plant container stock after last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; croton requires warm temperatures (above 60°F) at all times — avoid cold drafts, cold water, and cold soil, all of which cause leaf drop; in zones 9–11, it thrives as a year-round landscape shrub when protected from frost.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in bright indirect light to part sun; protect from direct afternoon sun in hot climates to prevent leaf scorch, but too little light causes diminished color intensity. Codiaeum variegatum (croton or garden croton) is one of the most spectacularly colored tropical foliage plants in cultivation — a Malaysian native producing thick, leathery leaves in an astonishing variety of shapes (lobed, ribbon-like, twisted, spiral, oak-leaf, or fiddle-shaped depending on variety) in colors ranging from bright yellow and orange to red, maroon, bronze, and multicolor combinations of all these, often changing color dramatically as leaves mature. No two leaves on an established plant may be the same color. Over 100 named cultivars exist including 'Mammy' (twisted red, orange, and yellow leaves), 'Petra' (classic red and yellow lobed leaves), and 'Gold Dust' (green leaves spotted yellow). In South Florida and zone 10+, croton grows as a perennial landscape shrub 3–8 feet tall; in zones 8–9, it is used as a summer patio plant and overwintered indoors. Croton sap is a skin irritant — use gloves when pruning. The sap and seeds are toxic if ingested.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Ornamentals

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; Lindheimer muhly is native to limestone outcrops and dry slopes in central Texas and is outstanding for hot, dry, alkaline, infertile conditions characteristic of central Texas caliche and limestone soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Lindheimer muhly is highly drought-tolerant once established and thrives in the calcareous, rocky soils of the Texas Hill Country. Muhlenbergia lindheimeri (Lindheimer muhly or big muhly) is a large-growing, clump-forming native grass endemic to central Texas limestone country, producing stately, upright clumps of blue-gray, fine-textured foliage that erupt in spectacular fall bloom — from September through November, the 4-to-6-foot clumps are topped with 12-to-18-inch, airy, silvery-blue to silver-gray flower plumes that catch and hold the light in autumn sun with extraordinary effect. Unlike the related and more widely known pink muhly (M. capillaris), Lindheimer muhly's plumes are silver-blue to whitish rather than pink-purple, creating a cooler, more elegant color effect. It is distinctly larger and more imposing than M. capillaris and is better suited to large landscape-scale plantings and mass drifts. In Texas Hill Country gardens and Gulf Coast landscapes, Lindheimer muhly is a landscape signature plant — combined with Texas sage (Leucophyllum), autumn sage (Salvia greggii), and native asters for a definitive Texas xeriscape composition.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ornamental Grasses

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in moist to wet, acidic (pH 4.5–6.5), well-drained to poorly drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; odorless bayberry is native to moist pine flatwoods, bay swamps, and wet pocosins of the Gulf Coastal Plain and tolerates brief to extended flooding better than most ornamental shrubs.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic, well-drained to occasionally flooded soil in full sun to part shade; Myrica inodora tolerates prolonged wet, acidic, poorly drained conditions that challenge most ornamental shrubs, making it excellent for wet spots, pond margins, and rain gardens. Myrica inodora (odorless bayberry) is a seldom-cultivated native shrub of the Gulf Coastal Plain from Alabama to Florida and Louisiana, distinct from the common Southern wax myrtle (Morella cerifera) in its larger, glossier, darker-green, distinctly odorless leaves (as the name implies — wax myrtle leaves are distinctively aromatic when crushed; Myrica inodora leaves are not). It grows as a large evergreen shrub or small tree with an upright, irregular form, producing small, white, clustered flowers in spring and blue-gray, waxy berries in fall and winter that are consumed by Yellow-rumped Warblers and other berry-eating birds. It is less commonly available in native plant nurseries than M. cerifera but is superior for wet, acidic sites in the Gulf South, where it grows with greater vigor than most other native shrubs.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Plants
